reason: The paper never names its importance metric explicitly (no mention of "Gini," "impurity," "permutation," "SHAP," or "VIMP" anywhere in the text), but the workflow (a ranger random survival forest, iterative reduction from 1,876 to 176 features, explicit note that "statistical significance testing imposes a substantial computation burden," and a bare "importance of predictors can be calculated on the basis of the model") is consistent with the fast, tree-split-based (impurity-type) importance rather than the far costlier repeated-permutation importance over ~1,900 candidate variables; no alternative rigorous importance method is ever cited. The magnitude/ranking of that importance is genuinely central to stated conclusions: variables are explicitly labeled "among the 20/50 most important predictors" and the Discussion and Conclusions build a pathogenesis narrative directly on this ranking ("The potential for the characteristics identified as important... to help predict time to clinical remission raise intriguing questions regarding the pathogenesis of CU," and Conclusion point 3: "provide insights into factors involved in the pathogenesis of the condition"). Corroboration is thin: only the single continuous variable age receives a partial dependence plot (Supplementary Fig. E1); the many binary/categorical top-ranked predictors (asthma, allergic rhinitis, chronic pulmonary disease, smoking status, LTRA/PPI/antihistamine use) receive no permutation importance, SHAP, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the selected feature set — the cohort-stratified summary tables merely describe how already-selected "important" features trend across outcome quartiles, which is circular and does not independently validate the ranking. Because impurity-type importance is plausibly used, its magnitude/ranking is load-bearing for a stated (if secondary) conclusion about disease pathogenesis, and corroboration is inadequate for all but one variable, the flag survives adversarial review.
